It proved that Bitcoin could complete settlements outside of the traditional payment system, but at the same time, it pushed Bitcoin into the context of drug trafficking, money laundering, and law enforcement concerns. A problem that the crypto industry has repeatedly encountered since 2011 was already beginning to take shape here: real demand does not naturally equal legitimate demand, and the fact that a transaction can occur does not mean that society will accept the way it occurs. In June, the EFF hit the brakes. After Silk Road propelled Bitcoin into the public eye, the hand that had reached out first in January began to withdraw. On June 20, 2011, the EFF announced on its official website that it would no longer accept Bitcoin donations. This action isn't a simple "change of heart." More accurately, it's like an organization deciding, after a meeting in its office, to remove a hot new button from its website. The article clearly states: EFF had been monitoring Bitcoin for several months and even tried accepting Bitcoin donations in an account set up for it by someone else. However, it later removed this donation option from its official help page. There are three reasons for this. First, the legal issues are too uncertain. Securities law, taxation, consumer protection, and money laundering issues still lack clear answers. EFF can defend users of new technologies, but it doesn't want to become the one testing the boundaries of the law. Second, how to spend the donations is also complicated. Donors give money hoping that charitable organizations will use it to support their work; but if the conversion of Bitcoin into cash is unclear, it will be difficult for the organization to explain. Third, it didn't want to create a misunderstanding: the EFF's acceptance of Bitcoin didn't equate to the EFF endorsing Bitcoin, much less implying that Bitcoin was safe, stable, or without investment risk. Most interestingly, the EFF didn't sell the received coins or return them to donors; instead, it decided to put them into the Bitcoin faucet, allowing them to continue circulating within the community. Many years later, companies, foundations, universities, merchants, listed companies, and payment institutions will face similar questions: Can cryptocurrencies be accepted? How should they be recorded on asset statements? How should they be valued in accounting? How can they be converted into fiat currency? These questions were already beginning to take shape with the EFF in 2011. Bitcoin hadn't even become a financial asset yet, and legal issues were already catching up. In June, Mt. Gox shut down. Silk Road brought Bitcoin into the media spotlight, and Mt. Gox dragged it back into the spotlight. On June 19, 2011, the price of Mt. Gox plummeted from around $17 to a few cents. Wired reported that day that the exchange suspended trading and directed visitors to a statement claiming the incident stemmed from a compromised account. Subsequently, user databases were leaked, and usernames, emails, and hashed passwords circulated online. The chain of events exposed by the incident was not complex. Mt. Gox was one of the main gateways for exchanging US dollars and Bitcoin at the time, and users entrusted their accounts, passwords, order books, and assets to the website. After the attack, the trading price plummeted on the platform, the exchange suspended services, and administrators attempted to roll back suspicious transactions. Subsequently, the user database was leaked, and usernames, email addresses, and hashed passwords began circulating online. The problem wasn't with the underlying Bitcoin ledger, but with the exchange system that users relied on to access the market. Mt. Gox eventually recovered in 2011, but it didn't truly cure its problems. In 2014, it collapsed in a more devastating way, entering bankruptcy proceedings, with many users waiting for compensation and liquidation for years. This story later leads to the tale of Jesse Powell and Kraken: Powell traveled to Japan to assist with the crisis management after the 2011 Mt. Gox incident and subsequently began building an exchange with a more central focus on security controls. Coinbase, after 2012, attempted to answer another question with a more internet-product-oriented approach: could ordinary people buy, sell, and store Bitcoin like they would a bank app? These companies are certainly not the simple opposite of Mt. Gox. They later encountered new problems such as regulation, custody, compliance, listing, user asset segregation, and market manipulation. However, from the perspective of industry evolution, the Mt. Gox incident did leave a shadow on later entrepreneurs: exchanges cannot simply be websites that facilitate buying and selling; they must handle security, risk control, auditing, customer assets, abnormal transactions, and external supervision like financial institutions. Many years later, cold and hot wallets, multi-signature permissions, internal approval, proof of reserves, compliance officers, on-chain risk control, judicial investigation, and custody licenses will become common terms in the industry. Users in 2011 didn't have these terms. They simply learned a basic truth on a bad night: the coins are on-chain, but that doesn't mean the risk is only on-chain. In June, 25,000 coins were stolen. Beyond exchanges, personal wallets also began to experience problems. In mid-June 2011, an early user named Allinvain posted on BitcoinTalk. The title was short but impactful: "I've just been hacked, any help is welcome, 25,000 BTC stolen." The imagery in the post was even more painful than the numbers. He said he woke up to find a large amount of Bitcoin had been transferred from his wallet to an unknown address. He tried to restore an earlier backup, but the transactions had already been confirmed. He suspected his computer or mining pool account had been compromised first, and then his wallet file was taken. The most heartbreaking part was roughly: "I meticulously backed up wallet.dat and encrypted it, but if someone has directly accessed my computer, all those efforts are useless." At the time, this was a loss in the hundreds of thousands of dollars. Wired later cautiously noted that while the veracity of this claim cannot be fully confirmed, it has certainly become one of the most representative stories in the early security history of Bitcoin. It made many people realize for the first time that owning their own money doesn't necessarily mean greater ease. Traditional bank accounts certainly have centralized problems. Accounts can be frozen, transactions can be audited, banks can make mistakes, and user privacy can be shared. But the banking system also provides some security buffers that ordinary people are already accustomed to: password recovery, abnormal transaction monitoring, customer service appeals, judicial freezing, account loss reporting, and risk control compensation. Bitcoin returns much power to users, but also pushes many responsibilities onto them. Your private key is in your hands, your assets are in your hands; if you lose your private key, your assets may be lost forever; a computer virus, copied wallet files, a weak password, or inadequate backups—the consequences are no longer just "changing your password." This system doesn't have a natural customer service center waiting to bail you out. Bitcoin's self-custodial ideal began to become burdensome in 2011. In the past, a wallet file was simply a file on a hard drive; after the price rose, it became like a gold bar without a safe. More troublesome, the problem wasn't limited to personal computers. Wired reviewed several early incidents: Bitomat in Poland, once the third-largest exchange, lost approximately 17,000 bitcoins after overwriting wallet files; the established wallet service MyBitcoin experienced prolonged outages, causing user panic. MyBitcoin later claimed it was hacked, but for users at the time, the more immediate problem was: I kept my coins in an online wallet, and now the provider isn't replying to emails. The problem with old finance was users' over-reliance on banks and payment institutions; the problem with new finance was users' belief that after getting rid of banks, any webpage could replace them. Bitcoin users in 2011 oscillated between these two extremes. On one end is self-custody, where you keep your private key and have no one to cover your losses if something goes wrong; on the other end is escrow, which is more convenient, but entrusts your trust to a website that may lack qualifications, auditing, regulation, or even transparency regarding your real identity. Back then, people didn't have the mature terms they would later use: cold and hot wallets, multi-signature permissions, escrow licenses, audit reports, on-chain risk control. Users only knew one thing: Bitcoin was truly valuable, but they didn't know how to safeguard it. In July, the myth of anonymity was debunked. In July 2011, a paper titled "An Analysis of Anonymity in the Bitcoin System" was published on arXiv. arXiv is a preprint platform for academic papers. This article is by Fergal Reid and Martin Harrigan. The two authors were from the Clique Research Cluster and Complex & Adaptive Systems Laboratory at University College Dublin, Ireland, and their research focused on network architecture, complex systems, and information network analysis. The paper points out that Bitcoin users identify themselves using public keys, and attackers can attempt to reconstruct the relationship between users and public keys. They also analyzed a Bitcoin theft incident, at the time valued at approximately $500,000, by combining on-chain structure and external information. The core conclusion of the paper is not complex: Bitcoin is not an absolutely anonymous system, but rather closer to a pseudonymous system on a public ledger. While addresses themselves don't display real names, anonymity becomes fragile once transaction relationships, fund flows, timelines, exchange deposits and withdrawals, forum posts, donation records, and real-world identities are linked to external information. This contrasts sharply with the story of the Silk Road. Black market users believed they were hiding behind anonymous access networks and Bitcoin addresses, but researchers saw another side: on-chain records don't disappear, and fund flows can be observed long-term. Traditional cash transactions are difficult to trace once completed; Bitcoin transactions, once on-chain, remain on the public ledger for a long time. Later, on-chain analytics companies, exchange compliance departments, law enforcement agencies, and judicial investigation services developed along this path. This 2011 paper was still just academic analysis, but it clearly stated a crucial fact: Bitcoin's anonymity isn't implicit; it depends on how users use addresses, exchanges, network tools, and real-world identities. If these clues are connected, the path of money can re-emerge as a personal story. In 2011, the story was just beginning. In October, mainstream media took notice. In October 2011, *The New Yorker* published Joshua Davis's long article, "The Crypto-Currency." This article brought Bitcoin into the narrative framework of mainstream American magazines. It not only introduced "what Bitcoin is," but also brought together several clues that had already surfaced at the time: Who is Satoshi Nakamoto? Can the code withstand scrutiny from security researchers? Why are merchants willing to accept Bitcoin? Legally, should it be considered currency, a commodity, or a security? The first clue was the search for Satoshi Nakamoto. Journalist Joshua Davis tracked down candidates like Michael Clear and Vili Lehdonvirta, but ultimately found no definitive answer. Michael Clear was young at the time, knowledgeable in cryptography, and had commented on the elegance and limitations of Bitcoin's design; Vili Lehdonvirta explicitly denied being Satoshi Nakamoto. This investigation, though inconclusive, made the "anonymous founder" a central mystery in Bitcoin's mainstream narrative. The second line of investigation is code scrutiny. The New Yorker reported that security researcher Dan Kaminsky attempted to find attack points in Bitcoin's code. Kaminsky was not an ordinary user; he was a well-known internet security researcher. He initially thought he could find vulnerabilities, but discovered that many attack paths had already been blocked by Satoshi Nakamoto. For Bitcoin, this kind of scrutiny meant it was no longer just a theoretical experiment in forums, but began to be publicly examined by external security experts. The third line of investigation is its legal and commercial attributes. The article mentions that some law professors believe Bitcoin exists in a gray area, unsure whether it should be considered currency, a commodity, or potentially a security; some merchants use Bitcoin as a payment tool to circumvent credit card fees and chargeback risks. By October 2011, Bitcoin was no longer just a tech company. The media, merchants, academics, security researchers, and regulators all began to treat it as a new financial phenomenon requiring explanation. In November, the price receded. Looking back at Bitcoin that year in November 2011, the price curve was no longer just a story of rising prices. Wired, in a lengthy article in November, recalled that from early April to the end of May 2011, the price of Bitcoin rose from less than $1 to $8.89; after the Silk Road report in early June, it surged again within a week, briefly approaching $27. The New Yorker's record shows an even higher peak: a single Bitcoin exceeded $29 in June, but by September it had fallen to $5. Wired, in that review, also wrote that the gold rush mining phase was ending, with some miners selling off modified mining rigs due to unbearable electricity costs, noise, and heat; meanwhile, those who remained more serious began to focus on infrastructure. Mt. Gox was researching point-of-sale hardware, other entrepreneurs were trying to create online merchant services similar to PayPal, and someone in Colorado created BitcoinDeals, aiming to sell millions of items using Bitcoin. After the price drop, what remained wasn't just losses and arguments, but also a group of people starting to push Bitcoin beyond the price narrative towards the more mundane aspects of payments, merchants, exchanges, and wallets. The 2011 downturn didn't push Bitcoin back to the obscure experiment of 2009. It has been rewritten simultaneously by the media, the black market, academics, exchanges, NGOs, and ordinary users. By the end of 2011, it was difficult to explain Bitcoin in a single sentence. It was like a post-financial crisis critique of the old monetary system, a settlement tool in the internet black market; like an open-source experiment by programmers, and a price game by speculators; it captivated digital rights organizations, yet quickly made them realize the legal implications. Many years later, the crypto industry will invent a whole host of mature terms: exchanges, wallets, mining pools, stablecoins, DeFi, RWA, on-chain risk control, custody licenses, travel rules, coin mixing sanctions, judicial investigations. But the old problems behind these terms had already surfaced in 2011. Bitcoin removes the ledger from institutional trust and entrusts it to a public network, cryptography, and consensus rules. But it doesn't remove people from the real world. People are still greedy, careless, speculative, and panicky; institutions still make mistakes, the media still amplifies conflicts, and governments still hold people accountable. Code can keep the ledger moving forward, but it can't suddenly make everyone working around the ledger reliable. Therefore, what's most memorable about 2011 isn't just Bitcoin reaching one dollar, nor just its rise to fame on the black market, nor just its first stumble at Mt. Gox. The bigger change is that Bitcoin, for the first time, simultaneously exists in two worlds: one world focused on hashes, private keys, blocks, computing power, and the longest chain; the other world focused on exchanges, drugs, donations, law enforcement, password leaks, user losses, and public opinion. In 2008, Satoshi Nakamoto proposed an electronic cash system that didn't require a trusted third party. In 2011, the real world gave it a harsher, but more truthful, answer: ledgers can have fewer intermediaries, but people will constantly create new intermediaries, thus testing trust once again. Bitcoin's 2011 quietly passed, leaving behind only two words: out of control.

Trump Earns $2.2 Billion in 2025, Large Proportion from Cryptocurrency Sector A financial document released on Tuesday, July 1st, reveals that US President Donald Trump's total income in 2025 will reach at least $2.2 billion, a significant increase from his minimum income of $622 million before his return to the White House in 2024. A substantial proportion of this new income comes from the cryptocurrency sector. Trump is the co-founder of his family business, World Liberty Financial, which has already generated $799 million in revenue, partly from funds purchased by the UAE government for its shares. Three days before his inauguration in January 2025, Trump also participated in the launch of the meme coin "$TRUMP". The financial disclosure shows that this project contributed $636 million in revenue, slightly higher than the total revenue from all other businesses in 2024. AI Hot Topics 1. Meta is selling its surplus AI computing resources Market News: Meta Platforms (META.O) is building a cloud business to sell its surplus AI computing resources. 2. Together AI announces completion of $800 million funding round, latest valuation reaches $8.3 billion On July 1, Together AI announced the completion of an $800 million funding round, with its latest valuation reaching $8.3 billion.

3. National Grid Invests $1.75 Billion in US AI Power Company

On July 1st, National Grid Plc, a London-listed power grid company with a large electricity and gas business in the northeastern United States, is taking a significant step to capitalize on the booming US data center industry by acquiring a stake in a company developing power generation facilities for Microsoft (MSFT.US). The utility will invest $1.75 billion to acquire a 35% stake in Joulent LLC. Joulent LLC is partnering with oil giant Chevron (CVX.US) to develop a natural gas power plant for Microsoft in Texas. Energy companies are broadly seeking investment opportunities in artificial intelligence through data center power projects. This deal values ​​the privately held company Joulent at $5 billion and will expand National Grid's business footprint from the northeastern United States to one of the hottest electricity markets in the country.

... 4. National Grid Invests $1.75 Billion in US AI Energy Company Jouleent On July 1st, according to market news, National Grid invested $1.75 billion in the US AI energy company Jouleent. 5. Apollo: AI Computing Power Shortage Worsens; Chips, Electricity, and Data Centers Become New "Strategic Resources" On July 1st, Apollo Asset Management pointed out in its latest report that the AI ​​industry is shifting from a "model race" to a "computing power competition." With the expansion of inference models and intelligent agent applications, the computational demand for a single task has increased significantly: intelligent agents repeatedly plan, retrieve, call tools, and verify results, and their token consumption can be 100 to 1000 times higher than that of traditional chatbot requests. The report argues that this shortage is not due to a single bottleneck, but rather a simultaneous pressure on GPUs, advanced manufacturing processes, storage, power, and grid access. Apollo states that the scarcity of computing power is revaluing assets, and the real competitive barrier will be the ability for companies to secure chip, storage, power, and data center resources in advance.

DeFi Hot Topics

1. Blockchain-based private lending platform Techdollar completes $3 million Pre-Seed funding round

On July 1st, according to official news, blockchain-based private lending platform Techdollar announced the completion of a $3 million Pre-Seed funding round. Investors include NoLimit Holdings, Reforge VC, and individual investors such as Newmichwill, RoyLearner, and Ansem. Techdollar is described as a blockchain-based private lending platform that allows founders, employees, venture capitalists, and family offices to obtain loans using equity in private technology companies as collateral, without the need to sell shares.

The platform employs a stablecoin issuance mechanism and integrates with on-chain liquidity infrastructure to provide rapid capital access, while maintaining private off-chain custody and compliant underwriting processes.

2. The Ventures team will join the crypto wallet Phantom

The official Phantom blog announced that Alvin Hsia, Emily Hsia, and Aris Samad will officially join Phantom and begin their onboarding this week. Alvin, Emily, and Aris are among the most respected developers in the Perpetual Futures (PERPS) space. Together, they built Ventures—one of the most mature trading products built on Hyperliquid, with an annualized trading volume exceeding $1 billion. The three previously worked at companies such as Airbnb, Brex, and Paradigm. They are precisely the experienced, high-level builders Phantom is looking to recruit, and will join the company's trading and data teams.

...

3. Polygon Founder: Over 107 Million POL Burned This Year, Network Officially Enters Deflationary State

On July 1st, Polygon founder Sandeep announced data on the X platform stating that since 2026, Polygon has minted approximately 105.2 million new POL, while approximately 107.7 million POL have been burned through the base fee mechanism. The amount burned exceeds the new issuance, and the Polygon network has officially achieved net deflation for the year. Sandeep added that the reduction in POL supply is mainly due to the burning of base fees resulting from Polygon's role as a payment chain handling high transaction volumes. In May, Polygon processed more transactions than other public chains, reaching 198 million transactions in a single month.

4. Total DeFi TVL Hits New Low Since February 2024

According to data from Defillama, the total value locked (TVL) in DeFi networks has fallen below $70 billion, currently standing at approximately $69.358 billion, marking a new low since February 2024.

5. Grayscale: Solana Has Become the Settlement Layer for Over 1,000 Applications, Processing Over 100 Million Transactions Daily This Year. On July 1st, Zach Pandl, Head of Research at Grayscale, stated that Solana has evolved into a blockchain network supporting large-scale applications, currently boasting over 1,000 decentralized applications (DApps). Since 2026, it has processed over 100 million transactions daily, averaging over 1,200 transactions per second, with approximately 4.3 million daily active users, contributing approximately $100 million in transaction fees to the network. Grayscale points out that the Solana ecosystem encompasses multiple areas including decentralized finance (DeFi), social trading, and decentralized infrastructure (DePIN). Among them, the Solana DEX, home to the decentralized exchange Raydium, has seen its cumulative trading volume exceed $360 billion this year; the Meme coin issuance platform Pump.fun has approximately 1.3 million monthly active users and daily revenue of about $690,000; and the DePIN project Geodnet focuses on providing centimeter-level positioning infrastructure for physical AI systems such as autonomous driving, robotics, and drones. Grayscale stated that Solana is continuously generating on-chain activity through its rich application ecosystem, and SOL provides investors with exposure to participate in the network's long-term growth.
